codeReview.summary: Solid MCP server implementation using JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio transport. Four tools (search_code, index_project, update_project, get_stats) are properly exposed with JSON Schema input definitions. Error resilience is well-tested: the server handles parse errors, invalid params, unknown tools, and internal errors without crashing. 28 unit tests cover protocol types, tool dispatch, and the server event loop. Code is clean, well-documented, and follows project conventions. The implementation uses stdio transport (the standard MCP approach) rather than the port-based HTTP transport mentioned in the feature description, which is actually the correct design choice for local tool servers. Several non-blocking issues identified around code duplication and services.yaml staleness.

issues.description: search_code always looks for the index in current_dir(), unlike index_project/update_project/get_stats which accept a 'path' parameter. This limits multi-project search scenarios. Consider adding an optional 'project_path' parameter to specify which project's index to search.

handle_index_project and handle_update_project (lines 233-340) share nearly identical code: parameter extraction, path validation, config creation, embedding provider setup, tokio runtime creation, and result serialization. Only the final function call differs (index_repository vs update_repository). A shared helper function would reduce ~50 lines of duplication.

try_hybrid_search duplicates significant logic from crates/vera-cli/src/commands/search.rs. Both implement BM25 fallback, embedding provider creation, reranker setup, and filter application. A shared search service function in vera-core would eliminate this cross-crate duplication and ensure behavior stays in sync.

A new tokio::runtime::Runtime is created for every tool call that needs async. While functional, this adds overhead per call. Consider creating the runtime once in run_server() and passing it to tool handlers.

The 'initialized' flag is set to true in the initialize handler (line 99) before notifications/initialized is received. Per strict MCP lifecycle spec, tools/list and tools/call should only be allowed after both initialize response AND notifications/initialized. Current behavior is permissive (works after just initialize). Most MCP clients won't exploit this, but it's a minor protocol deviation.

File is 489 lines, exceeding the 300-line soft target from AGENTS.md. The tool definitions (~80 lines of JSON schema data) inflate the count. Consider extracting tool definitions to a separate file or using a macro/const approach to bring this file under the soft target.
File is 452 lines, exceeding the 300-line soft target from AGENTS.md. About half the file is tests, which is reasonable. The production code portion (~190 lines) is well within limits.

sharedStateObservations.observation: services.yaml has an MCP service entry with 'start: VERA_LOG=info cargo run -- mcp --port 3200', HTTP health check ('curl -sf http://localhost:3200/health'), and 'lsof -ti :3200'-based stop command. However, the actual MCP server implementation is stdio-based (reads from stdin, writes to stdout) with no --port flag, no HTTP listener, and no health check endpoint. The services.yaml entry is stale/incorrect and would cause failures if any agent tries to use it.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: .factory/services.yaml lines 10-16 vs crates/vera-cli/src/commands/mcp.rs (stdio-only implementation) and crates/vera-mcp/src/server.rs (no port/HTTP logic)

sharedStateObservations.observation: The MCP tools.rs reimplements hybrid search logic that already exists in the CLI's commands/search.rs. This creates a pattern where search behavior could diverge between 'vera search' and MCP 'search_code' if one is updated but not the other. The architecture library (architecture.md) doesn't document a convention for shared service layers between CLI and MCP interfaces.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: crates/vera-mcp/src/tools.rs try_hybrid_search (lines 130-200) vs crates/vera-cli/src/commands/search.rs run (lines 15-115) — nearly identical logic for BM25 fallback, embedding provider setup, reranker creation, and filter application

sharedStateObservations.observation: Feature description specified 'starts server on configured port (default 3200)' implying HTTP transport, but worker implemented stdio transport instead. While stdio is the correct standard MCP transport and arguably a better design choice, this deviation from the feature spec wasn't flagged in the handoff. The skillFeedback.followedProcedure was marked true with no deviations noted.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: features.json description: 'vera mcp starts server on configured port (default 3200)' vs actual implementation: stdio transport with no port concept

summary: The MCP server implementation passes review. The core functionality is solid: JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio transport with proper initialize handshake, 4 well-defined tools (search_code, index_project, update_project, get_stats), comprehensive error handling, and strong test coverage (28 tests). Error resilience is thoroughly verified — the server continues operating after parse errors, invalid params, and internal failures. The main concerns are non-blocking: (1) services.yaml is stale with incorrect port/HTTP config that doesn't match the stdio implementation, (2) significant code duplication between MCP tool handlers and CLI commands that could diverge over time, and (3) search_code lacks a project_path parameter, limiting multi-project scenarios. All 7 expectedBehavior items from the feature spec are met, with the caveat that the feature description mentioned port 3200 but stdio transport was implemented instead (which is actually the standard MCP approach).
